entries.title: On Matt 15:12–14
entries.excerpt: In one of the Lord’s discourses the whole superstition of Jewish observances had been cut down. They placed their whole religion in using or abstaining from certain meats.

entries.title: On Matt 15:12–14
entries.excerpt: When the Pharisees heard the things that went before, they made no reply to them, because He had so mightily overthrown them, not only refuting their arguments, but detecting their fraud, but they, not the multitudes, were offended at them. “Then came his disciples unto him and said, Knowest thou that the Pharisees were offended after they heard this saying?”

entries.title: On Matt 15:12–14
entries.excerpt: As this word ‘scandalum’ (offence or stumblingblock) is of such frequent use in ecclesiastical writings, we will shortly explain it. We might render it in Latin, ‘offendiculum,’ or ‘ruina,’ or ‘impactio;’ and so when we read, Whosoever shall scandalize, we understand, whoso by word or deed has given an occasion of falling to any.

entries.title: On Matt 15:12–14
entries.excerpt: Christ does not remove the stumblingblock out of the way of the Pharisees, but rather rebukes them; as it follows, “But he answered and said, Every plant which my heavenly Father has not planted shall be rooted up.” This Manichaeus affirmed was spoken of the Law, but what has been already said is a sufficient refutation of this. For if He had said this of the Law, how would He have above contended for the Law, saying, “Why transgress ye the commandment of God through your tradition?” Or would He have cited the Prophet? Or how, if God said, “Honour thy father and thy mother,” is not this,…

entries.title: On Matt 15:12–14
entries.excerpt: Shall that plant also be rooted up of which the Apostle says, “I planted, Apollos watered?” 1 Cor 3:6 The question is answered by what follows, “but God gave the increase.” He says also, “Ye are God’s husbandry, a building of God;” and in another place, “We are workers together of God.” And if when Paul plants, and Apollos waters, they are in so doing workers together with God, then God plants and waters together with them. This passage is abused by some who apply it at once to two different kinds of men; they say, ‘If every plant which the Father hath not planted shall be rooted up, then…

entries.title: On Matt 15:12–14
entries.excerpt: This is also the same as that Apostolic injunction, “A heretic after the first and second admonition reject, knowing that such a one is perverse.” Tit 3:11-11 To the same end the Saviour commands evil teachers to be left to their own will, knowing that it is hardly that they can be brought to the truth.

entries.title: On Matt 15:12–14
entries.excerpt: What He intends then by a plant not planted of His Father, is that tradition of men under cover557 of which the Law had been transgressed, this He instructs them must be rooted up.

entries.title: On Matt 15:12–14
entries.excerpt: Every false doctrine and superstitious observance with the workers thereof cannot endure; and because it is not from God the Father, it shall be rooted up with the same. And that only shall endure which is of God.

entries.title: On Matt 15:12–14
entries.excerpt: They are blind, that is, they want the light of God’s commandments; and they are “leaders of the blind,” inasmuch as they draw others headlong, erring, and leading into error; whence it is added, “If the blind lead the blind, they both fall into the ditch.”

entries.title: On Matt 15:12–14
entries.excerpt: Or, the plant here spoken of may be the doctors of the Law with their followers, who had not Christ for their foundation. Why they are to be rooted up, He adds, “Let them alone; they are blind, leaders of the blind.”
